definition: Application Programming Interface — a defined way for two software systems to talk to each other.
Representational State Transfer — a common style for building web APIs over HTTP using URLs and verbs like GET and POST.
Continuous Integration / Continuous Delivery — automatically building, testing, and shipping code as it changes.
An open-source system for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications.
A tool that packages software into lightweight, portable containers that run the same everywhere.
Service Level Agreement — a commitment about how reliable or fast a service will be (e.g. 99.9% uptime).
Service Level Objective — the internal target that a team aims for to meet an SLA.
Key Performance Indicator — a metric used to judge whether something is on track.
Objectives and Key Results — a goal-setting framework pairing an ambitious objective with measurable results.
Minimum Viable Product — the smallest version of a product that can be released to learn from real users.
Software as a Service — software delivered over the internet on a subscription rather than installed locally.
Business to Business — selling products or services to other companies rather than to consumers.
Business to Consumer — selling directly to individual end users.
Structured Query Language — the standard language for querying and managing relational databases.
Object-Relational Mapping — a layer that lets code work with database rows as objects.
Test-Driven Development — writing automated tests before the code that makes them pass.
Create, Read, Update, Delete — the four basic operations on stored data.
The delay between a request and its response, usually measured in milliseconds.
How much work a system handles per unit of time, e.g. requests per second.
An operation that has the same effect whether it runs once or many times.
A system's ability to handle growing load by adding resources.
An iterative way of working that delivers software in short cycles with frequent feedback.
Anyone with an interest in a project's outcome — users, customers, leadership, or partners.
Return on Investment — the gain from an action relative to its cost.
The rate at which customers stop using a product over a period of time.
General Data Protection Regulation — the EU law governing how personal data is collected and handled.
Building systems that learn patterns from data instead of being explicitly programmed.
Large Language Model — an AI model trained on vast text that generates and understands natural language.
